overview.whyItMatters: Calling foundation models correctly is fundamental. Understanding synchronous vs asynchronous patterns, streaming responses, error handling, and intelligent routing determines whether your application is reliable and performant or frustrating and flaky.

concepts.title: Synchronous and Asynchronous FM Calls
concepts.content:
FM API calls can be synchronous (wait for response) or asynchronous (submit and retrieve later). The choice impacts user experience, system architecture, and cost.

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    FM API Call Patterns                              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                      │
│  SYNCHRONOUS          ASYNCHRONOUS           STREAMING               │
│  ────────────         ─────────────          ─────────               │
│                                                                      │
│  Client               Client                 Client                  │
│    │                    │                      │                     │
│    ▼                    ▼                      ▼                     │
│  Request ──────▶    Submit ──────▶         Request ──────▶          │
│    │                    │                      │                     │
│   ~~~                Return                    ▼                     │
│  WAIT               job_id                 Token 1                   │
│   ~~~                   │                      ▼                     │
│    │                 Poll or                Token 2                   │
│    ▼                Callback                   ▼                     │
│  Response               │                   Token N                   │
│    │                    ▼                      ▼                     │
│    ▼                Response                [DONE]                   │
│  Done                   │                      │                     │
│                         ▼                      ▼                     │
│                       Done                   Done                    │
│                                                                      │
│  Latency:           Latency:               Latency:                  │
│  High (wait)        Decoupled              Fast TTFB                 │
│                                                                      │
│  Use: Simple        Use: Batch             Use: Chat UI              │
│  API calls          Background             Real-time                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Synchronous (Bedrock InvokeModel):**
The caller waits while the model processes. Simple to implement. Works for interactive use cases where users expect immediate responses. Lambda, EC2, ECS, or any compute can call Bedrock synchronously.

Limitations: Long inference times (10+ seconds for complex prompts) can cause timeouts. API Gateway has a 29-second limit. Users waiting for responses may abandon.

**Asynchronous Patterns:**
Submit requests, get results later. Implement with SQS queues: producers submit requests, Lambda consumers process them, results go to another queue or storage.

Bedrock doesn't have native async invoke, but you build async patterns around it. SQS decouples submission from processing. Step Functions orchestrates multi-step async workflows.

**Batch Inference:**
Process many requests together for efficiency. SageMaker batch transform jobs process files of inputs. Useful for bulk processing where real-time response isn't needed: document analysis, data enrichment, offline processing.
keyPoints: InvokeModel: Synchronous, caller waits for response
SQS + Lambda: Build async patterns for background processing
Batch inference: Process many requests together for efficiency
Match pattern to latency tolerance and use case

concepts.title: Streaming FM Responses
keyPoints: SSE is simplest for server-to-client streaming
Buffer when you need to validate/filter before display

concepts.title: Building Resilient FM Integrations
concepts.content:
FM APIs can fail, timeout, or be rate-limited. Production applications need resilience patterns to handle these gracefully.

**Exponential Backoff:**
AWS SDK implements this automatically. When requests fail with retriable errors, wait and retry with increasing delays. Prevents thundering herd problems during outages.

**Rate Limiting:**
Bedrock and SageMaker have account-level rate limits. API Gateway can enforce application-level limits to prevent single users from consuming all capacity. Use usage plans and API keys for per-client limits.

**Circuit Breakers:**
When a service is failing consistently, stop trying. Circuit breaker opens after N failures, fast-fails requests for a cooldown period, then tries again. Prevents wasting resources on known-failing services.

**Fallback Strategies:**
- Return cached responses for similar queries
- Fall back to a different model (if Sonnet fails, try Haiku)
- Graceful degradation: return partial results or apologetic messages
- Queue for retry: accept the request, process when service recovers

**X-Ray Tracing:**
Distributed tracing across services. See where requests spend time, identify bottlenecks, debug failures. Essential for understanding performance in complex GenAI pipelines.
keyPoints: Exponential backoff: Built into AWS SDK for retriable errors
API Gateway rate limiting: Prevent capacity exhaustion
Circuit breakers: Stop calling failing services
Fallback: Cache, alternative models, graceful degradation
concepts.examTip: 'Resilient' or 'handle failures gracefully' -> exponential backoff, circuit breakers, fallback strategies.

concepts.title: Intelligent Request Routing
concepts.content:
Not all requests should go to the same model or endpoint. Intelligent routing directs requests based on content, cost, performance, or other criteria.

**Static Routing:**
Predefined rules route requests. API Gateway routes based on path (/simple goes to Haiku, /complex goes to Sonnet). Simple to implement, but inflexible.

**Content-Based Dynamic Routing:**
Analyze request content to determine routing. Step Functions orchestrates classification then routing. A small, fast classifier determines complexity, then routes to appropriate model.

**Metric-Based Routing:**
Route based on current system state. If primary endpoint is overloaded (high latency, errors), route to secondary. CloudWatch metrics trigger routing changes through Lambda or Step Functions.

**API Gateway Request Transformation:**
Transform requests before they reach backends. Add system prompts, inject context, format for specific model requirements. Keeps transformation logic centralized.

**Use Cases:**
- Cost optimization: Route simple queries to cheap models
- Performance: Route latency-sensitive requests to provisioned throughput
- Compliance: Route sensitive data to specific regions or endpoints
- A/B testing: Route percentage of traffic to test new models or prompts
keyPoints: Content-based: Classify then route to appropriate model
Metric-based: Route based on system health/load
Step Functions orchestrates complex routing logic

skillExplanations.explanation:
Synchronous Bedrock calls work from any compute: Lambda, EC2, ECS, Fargate, even client applications with proper credentials. The AWS SDK handles authentication, serialization, and transport.

AWS SDK best practices: Use the latest SDK version. Enable retry configuration for transient failures. Set appropriate timeouts (model inference can take 10-30 seconds for complex prompts).

API Gateway adds a managed layer: authentication, throttling, caching, monitoring. For production APIs, front Bedrock calls with API Gateway. Validate inputs before they reach the model.

awsServices.whenToUse: InvokeModel API for synchronous FM calls from any compute.
Front-end for Bedrock APIs. Validation, throttling, authentication.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Asynchronous patterns decouple request submission from processing. SQS queues buffer requests. Lambda functions consume and process. Results go to output queues, SNS notifications, or storage.

Batch inference with SageMaker processes files of inputs. Upload inputs to S3, run batch transform job, results appear in S3. No real-time response, but efficient for bulk processing.

When to use async: Background processing, bulk operations, workloads that tolerate delays, protecting downstream services from traffic spikes.

awsServices.whenToUse: Queue-based async processing. Decouple submission from inference.
Process queue messages with InvokeModel in Lambda consumers.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Streaming APIs return tokens incrementally. InvokeModelWithResponseStream opens a stream that delivers chunks as tokens are generated. Processing each chunk updates the user interface in real-time.

SSE (Server-Sent Events) is the standard for server-to-client streaming over HTTP. The client opens a connection; the server sends events. Works through proxies, simpler than WebSockets. API Gateway supports SSE through chunked transfer encoding.

WebSockets enable bidirectional streaming but are more complex. Use when you need client-to-server streaming during inference (rare for FM use cases).

awsServices.whenToUse: Proxy streaming responses to clients. WebSocket APIs for bidirectional.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Resilience requires multiple strategies working together. AWS SDK provides exponential backoff automatically. You configure retry counts and add circuit breakers.

API Gateway rate limiting prevents capacity exhaustion. Set account-level service quotas. Use usage plans for per-client limits. Return 429 (Too Many Requests) rather than failing completely.

X-Ray traces requests across services. Instrument Lambda functions and SDK calls. Trace maps show request flow and identify slow components. Essential for debugging production issues.

awsServices.whenToUse: Distributed tracing for debugging and performance analysis.
SDK with retry configuration for resilient FM calls.

skillExplanations.explanation:
Intelligent routing optimizes cost, performance, and reliability. Static routing uses API Gateway path-based routing or Lambda function logic. Simple but requires manual configuration.

Dynamic routing makes decisions at runtime. Classify request complexity, check endpoint health, consider cost constraints. Step Functions orchestrates this decision logic cleanly.

API Gateway request transformations modify requests before routing. Add system prompts, inject user context, format for model requirements. Centralizes transformation logic in the gateway.

awsServices.whenToUse: Orchestrate content-based and metric-based routing decisions.
Multiple models as routing targets for different query types.

values.Server-Sent Events (SSE): Streaming FM responses to web clients
values.WebSockets: Interactive chat with user input during streaming
values.Polling: Legacy clients that can't use SSE/WS

examStrategies.approach: InvokeModelWithResponseStream + SSE or WebSocket through API Gateway. SSE is simpler for server-to-client streaming. WebSocket only if bidirectional is needed.
Exponential backoff (SDK default), circuit breakers, fallback to alternative models or cached responses. X-Ray for debugging failures.
Step Functions for content-based routing. API Gateway for path-based static routing. CloudWatch metrics for health-based routing.

keyTakeaways: InvokeModel for sync, SQS + Lambda for async, InvokeModelWithResponseStream for streaming
SSE is the standard for streaming FM responses to web clients
Exponential backoff + circuit breakers + fallbacks = resilient integration
X-Ray traces requests across services for debugging
Step Functions orchestrates intelligent content-based routing
commonMistakes: Not using streaming for user-facing applications (poor UX)
Missing timeout configuration (default may be too short for FM)
No fallback strategy when FM calls fail
Polling instead of SSE/WebSocket for real-time updates
Hardcoding model selection instead of intelligent routing
